docs.title: Welcome to Semiconductor Physics
docs.text: An interactive university-level semiconductor physics textbook with AI explanations, quizzes, simulations, and practice problems.

• index.md is the home page

  • course-description.md is the file used to generate the learning graph for the course

docs.title: Chapters
docs.text: This section contains the main chapter content of the textbook. Every chapter has its own folder with a two-digit number at the start of the directory name.

docs.title: About This Book
docs.text: Semiconductor Physics is an intelligent textbook designed for junior-level Electrical Engineering and Applied Physics undergraduates. It covers the quantum-mechanical and statistical foundations of semiconductor materials and devices, from crystal structure through the p-n junction and MOS capacitor.

docs.title: Author
docs.text: Natol Dinka

docs.text:

  • Claude Code - AI-powered development assistant

docs.title: Semiconductor Physics
docs.text:
Title: Semiconductor Physics

Target Audience: College undergraduate — junior-level Electrical Engineering and Applied/Engineering Physics majors

Prerequisites:

  • Calculus-based general physics (mechanics and electricity & magnetism)
  • Ordinary differential equations
  • Introductory modern/quantum physics (wave-particle duality, the Schrödinger equation at an introductory level)
  • Basic linear circuit analysis (Ohm's law, Kirchhoff's laws) is helpful but not required

docs.title: Course Overview
docs.text:
Semiconductor Physics introduces the quantum-mechanical and statistical foundations that explain how semiconductor materials behave and how that behavior is engineered into the diodes, transistors, and integrated circuits that underlie modern electronics. Students move from the atomic structure of crystalline solids, through energy band theory and carrier statistics, to the transport and junction physics that govern real devices.

This course bridges introductory physics and device-level electrical engineering. Where a circuits course treats a diode or transistor as a black-box component defined by its terminal behavior, this course opens the box: it derives why a p-n junction rectifies, why doping controls conductivity over many orders of magnitude, and why a MOS capacitor can invert its surface charge. This physical grounding is essential for anyone who will later design, fabricate, model, or push the limits of semiconductor devices — from power electronics to microprocessors to solar cells.

The course emphasizes building physical intuition alongside quantitative modeling: students learn to read and construct band diagrams, apply carrier statistics to predict conductivity and Fermi level position, and derive the current-voltage behavior of a p-n junction from first principles.

docs.title: Main Topics Covered
docs.text:
• Crystal structure and bonding in solids (unit cells, Miller indices, covalent bonding in silicon/germanium)
  • Quantum mechanics foundations for solids (Schrödinger equation, particle in a box, tunneling)
  • Energy band theory (Kronig-Penney model, formation of bands and band gaps, direct vs. indirect gap materials)
  • Semiconductors in thermal equilibrium (intrinsic vs. extrinsic material, donors and acceptors, ionization)
  • Carrier statistics (density of states, Fermi-Dirac distribution, Fermi level position, carrier concentration equations)
  • Carrier transport (drift, mobility, conductivity, diffusion, the Einstein relation)
  • Non-equilibrium excess carriers (optical/thermal generation, recombination mechanisms, minority carrier lifetime, continuity equations)
  • The p-n junction (built-in potential, depletion approximation, junction capacitance, the ideal diode equation)
  • Metal-semiconductor junctions (Schottky barriers vs. ohmic contacts)
  • The MOS capacitor and semiconductor surfaces (accumulation, depletion, inversion — the physical basis of the MOSFET)
  • Optical and thermal properties of semiconductors (absorption, photogeneration, photodiodes/solar cells as an application)

docs.title: Topics Not Covered
docs.text:
• Semiconductor device fabrication and process technology (photolithography, ion implantation, etch/deposition processes)
  • Full transistor circuit design and analog/digital circuit theory (covered in a separate circuits/devices course)
  • Advanced quantum transport and mesoscopic/nanoscale device physics (ballistic transport, quantum dots, spintronics)
  • TCAD and detailed numerical device simulation tools
  • Compound and exotic semiconductor material systems in depth (III-V heterostructures, wide-bandgap materials) beyond brief mention
  • Semiconductor manufacturing economics and industry/business topics

docs.title: Learning Outcomes
docs.text: After completing this course, students will be able to:

docs.title: Remember
docs.text:
Retrieving, recognizing, and recalling relevant knowledge from long-term memory.

  • State the postulates of quantum mechanics relevant to solids and define key terms (wavefunction, eigenstate, energy band, band gap)
  • Recall the standard notation and typical values for silicon material properties (band gap, intrinsic carrier concentration, effective mass, dielectric constant)
  • List the types of dopant atoms used to create n-type and p-type silicon and identify their group in the periodic table
  • Identify the defining equations for drift current, diffusion current, and the ideal diode current-voltage relationship

docs.title: Understand
docs.text:
Constructing meaning from instructional messages, including oral, written, and graphic communication.

  • Explain, in physical terms, why crystalline solids form energy bands rather than discrete atomic energy levels
  • Explain the difference between intrinsic and extrinsic semiconductors and how doping shifts the Fermi level
  • Describe the physical origin of the built-in potential at a p-n junction and interpret an equilibrium band diagram
  • Summarize the roles of generation and recombination in determining minority carrier concentration under illumination or injection

docs.title: Apply
docs.text:
Carrying out or using a procedure in a given situation.

  • Apply the Fermi-Dirac distribution and density-of-states function to calculate electron and hole concentrations at a given temperature and doping level
  • Use the mass-action law and charge neutrality condition to solve for majority and minority carrier concentrations in doped semiconductors
  • Apply drift-diffusion equations to compute current density in a semiconductor under an applied field and/or concentration gradient
  • Calculate the built-in potential, depletion width, and junction capacitance of a p-n junction from doping concentrations

docs.title: Analyze
docs.text:
Breaking material into constituent parts and determining how the parts relate to one another and to an overall structure or purpose.

  • Analyze a semiconductor band diagram to determine doping type, relative doping level, and bias condition
  • Differentiate between direct-gap and indirect-gap semiconductors and analyze the implications for optical absorption and emission
  • Analyze the continuity equation for excess minority carriers to determine steady-state carrier profiles near a junction or surface
  • Compare drift-dominated versus diffusion-dominated transport regimes in a given device structure

docs.title: Evaluate
docs.text:
Making judgments based on criteria and standards through checking and critiquing.

  • Evaluate whether the depletion (abrupt-junction) approximation is appropriate for a given p-n junction and justify the assessment
  • Critique a proposed doping profile or material choice against target electrical specifications (e.g., breakdown voltage, switching speed)
  • Assess the trade-offs between direct and indirect band gap materials for a specific optoelectronic application (LED vs. silicon photodiode)
  • Judge the validity of simplifying assumptions (e.g., low-level injection, quasi-neutral regions) used in a given device derivation

docs.title: Create
docs.text:
Putting elements together to form a coherent or functional whole; reorganizing elements into a new pattern or structure.

  • Derive the ideal diode equation from the minority carrier diffusion equations and boundary conditions at a p-n junction
  • Design a doping profile (type and concentration) to achieve a target built-in potential, depletion width, or breakdown voltage
  • Construct a complete equilibrium and non-equilibrium band diagram for a novel junction structure given its doping and bias
  • Capstone project: Model and report on the physics of a real semiconductor device (e.g., solar cell, LED, or power diode), including band diagrams, carrier concentration calculations, and derivation of its key I-V or efficiency characteristic, connecting the physics to a real-world performance metric

docs.title: Course Importance
docs.text: Semiconductor physics is the physical foundation underneath nearly every modern electronic and photonic technology — microprocessors, memory, power converters, solar cells, LEDs, and sensors. Engineers who understand the underlying physics can reason about why devices behave as they do, diagnose failures at a fundamental level, and contribute to the design of next-generation devices, rather than treating components purely as circuit-level abstractions. This course is typically a prerequisite for upper-division device and VLSI courses and for research or industry work in semiconductor devices, photonics, and nanotechnology.

docs.title: Chapters
docs.text: This textbook is organized into 18 chapters covering 200 concepts.

docs.title: Chapter Overview
docs.text:
1. Physics and Math Foundations - Reviews electrostatics, thermal physics, and photon concepts needed for later chapters.
  2. Quantum Mechanics Foundations - Introduces wave-particle duality, the Schrödinger equation, and simple quantum systems.
  3. Crystal Lattices and Structures - Covers crystal lattices, unit cells, lattice types, and Miller indices.
  4. Chemical Bonding in Semiconductor Crystals - Covers covalent, ionic, and metallic bonding and tetrahedral bonding in silicon.
  5. Quantum Mechanics of Periodic Crystals - Derives how a periodic crystal potential produces energy bands.
  6. Band Structure and the Fermi Level - Covers direct/indirect band gaps, effective mass, density of states, and the Fermi level.
  7. Intrinsic and Extrinsic Semiconductors - Introduces intrinsic vs. extrinsic material and donor/acceptor doping.
  8. Doping, Ionization, and Temperature Regimes - Covers n-type/p-type doping, dopant ionization, and temperature regions.
  9. Carrier Concentration Statistics - Introduces free electrons, holes, and the Fermi-Dirac distribution.
  10. Fermi Level Position and Carrier Equations - Derives electron/hole concentration equations and locates the Fermi level.
  11. Drift Current and Carrier Mobility - Covers drift and diffusion current, mobility, scattering, and conductivity.
  12. Diffusion and Advanced Transport Phenomena - Covers the Hall effect, the Einstein relation, and advanced transport behavior.
  13. Non-Equilibrium Carriers and Recombination - Introduces excess carrier generation/recombination and the continuity equation.
  14. The P-N Junction at Equilibrium - Builds the equilibrium p-n junction: depletion region, built-in potential, and capacitance.
  15. The P-N Junction Under Bias - Covers forward/reverse bias, the ideal diode equation, and breakdown.
  16. Metal-Semiconductor and MOS Junctions - Covers Schottky barriers, ohmic contacts, and the MOS capacitor.
  17. Optical and Thermal Properties of Semiconductors - Covers light absorption and optoelectronic devices.
  18. Semiconductor Devices and Applications - Synthesizes applied devices and the capstone device project.

docs.title: How to Use This Textbook
docs.text:
Chapters are ordered so that every concept appears after all of its prerequisites — work through them in numerical order on a first pass. Each chapter's "Prerequisites" section lists the specific earlier chapters it builds on, so if you already have a strong background in a topic (e.g., quantum mechanics or crystal structure), you can use those links to check what you can skim.

Note: Each chapter includes a list of concepts covered. Make sure to complete prerequisites before moving to advanced chapters.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This chapter reviews the electrostatics, thermal physics, and photon concepts that the rest of the course builds on. Students revisit Coulomb's law, electric field and potential, thermal equilibrium, and the concept of photon energy. These ideas are the physical vocabulary used throughout the course to describe forces, energy, and thermal behavior inside a semiconductor crystal.

docs.title: Concepts Covered
docs.text:
This chapter covers the following 8 concepts from the learning graph:

  1. Coulomb's Law
  2. Electric Field
  3. Electric Potential
  4. Electrostatic Potential Energy
  5. Kinetic Theory of Gases
  6. Boltzmann Constant
  7. Thermal Equilibrium
  8. Photon Energy

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This chapter introduces the quantum-mechanical concepts required to understand solids: wave-particle duality, the Heisenberg uncertainty principle, and the Schrödinger equation. Students work through simple quantum systems — the particle in a box, potential wells, and tunneling — that establish the mathematical tools used later to derive energy bands.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This chapter introduces the geometric structure of crystalline solids: lattices, unit cells, and the common cubic and diamond-family lattice structures found in semiconductor materials. Students learn to describe crystal planes using Miller indices, a tool used throughout the course to discuss crystal orientation and surfaces.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This chapter explains how atoms bond together to form crystalline solids, covering covalent, ionic, and metallic bonding. Students see how silicon's tetrahedral covalent bonding, built from its valence electrons, gives rise to the diamond lattice structure introduced in the previous chapter, and briefly considers amorphous, polycrystalline, and defective crystal forms.

  8. Polycrystalline Solid
  9. Crystal Defect

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This chapter applies quantum mechanics to a periodic crystal potential, using Bloch's theorem and the Kronig-Penney model to show how discrete atomic energy levels broaden into continuous energy bands. Students learn the reciprocal lattice and Brillouin zone concepts used to describe these bands, and see how allowed and forbidden energy regions emerge from the crystal's periodicity.

  11. Allowed Energy States

docs.title: Band Structure and the Fermi Level
Summary
docs.text: This chapter distinguishes direct and indirect band-gap materials using the E-k diagram, and introduces effective mass and density of states as tools for describing carriers within a band. Students learn the Fermi level and Fermi energy, and use band structure to classify materials as metals, insulators, semiconductors, or semimetals.

  6. Fermi Level

  8. Metal Band Structure
  9. Insulator Band Structure
  10. Semiconductor Band Structure
  11. Semimetal

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This chapter introduces the distinction between intrinsic (pure) and extrinsic (doped) semiconductors, and surveys the elemental and compound semiconductor materials used in practice, including silicon, germanium, and gallium arsenide. Students learn how donor and acceptor atoms are introduced into the crystal lattice, setting up the doping concepts covered in the next chapter.

  7. Gallium Arsenide

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This chapter covers how donor and acceptor doping creates n-type and p-type material, and how dopant ionization energy determines when those dopants actually contribute free carriers. Students learn the freeze-out, extrinsic, and intrinsic temperature regions that describe how a doped semiconductor's behavior changes with temperature, along with compensated and degenerate doping.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This chapter introduces free electrons and holes as the two carrier types in a semiconductor, and applies the Fermi-Dirac distribution and density of states function to describe how carriers populate energy states. Students derive the effective density of states and the intrinsic carrier concentration, and use the mass action law and charge neutrality condition to relate carrier populations to doping.

docs.title: Fermi Level Position and Carrier Equations
Summary
docs.text: This chapter uses the Boltzmann approximation to simplify carrier statistics in nondegenerate semiconductors, deriving working equations for electron and hole concentration. Students learn how the Fermi level position shifts with doping and temperature, and locate the intrinsic Fermi level as a reference point, arriving at the general carrier concentration equations used throughout the rest of the course.

  7. Intrinsic Fermi Level
  8. Carrier Concentration Equation

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This chapter introduces drift current — the current caused by an applied electric field — and diffusion current — the current caused by a carrier concentration gradient — along with carrier mobility, drift velocity, and the scattering mechanisms (lattice and impurity scattering) that limit mobility. Students use these ideas to derive conductivity, resistivity, and sheet resistance, the practical transport quantities used to characterize a doped semiconductor.

docs.text:

  6. Lattice Scattering
  7. Impurity Scattering
  8. Conductivity
  9. Resistivity
  10. Sheet Resistance

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This chapter builds out the transport picture with the Hall effect (and its coefficient and voltage), the diffusion coefficient, and the Einstein relation that connects diffusion to mobility. Students learn Fick's law and concentration gradients, combine drift and diffusion into a total current density expression, and see how velocity saturation and temperature affect carrier mobility at high fields and elevated temperatures.

docs.title: Concepts Covered
docs.text:
This chapter covers the following 10 concepts from the learning graph:

  3. Hall Voltage
  4. Diffusion Coefficient
  5. Einstein Relation
  6. Fick's Law
  7. Concentration Gradient
  8. Total Current Density
  9. Velocity Saturation
  10. Mobility Temperature Dependence

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This chapter moves beyond thermal equilibrium to describe excess carriers created by optical or thermal generation, and the recombination mechanisms — direct, indirect, trap-assisted (Shockley-Read-Hall), and Auger — that return a semiconductor to equilibrium. Students learn minority carrier lifetime, low- and high-level injection, and the continuity equation, arriving at the quasi-Fermi levels and steady-state carrier profiles used to analyze biased devices.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This chapter builds the p-n junction from first principles at thermal equilibrium, starting from the metallurgical junction and Poisson's equation, through the depletion approximation, to the built-in potential and depletion charge density. Students calculate the junction electric field, depletion width, and junction capacitance — the equilibrium quantities needed before introducing bias in the next chapter.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This chapter analyzes the p-n junction away from equilibrium, covering forward and reverse bias, minority carrier injection, and the short-base and long-base diode approximations. Students derive the saturation current and the ideal diode equation, then study reverse breakdown, including avalanche and Zener mechanisms, and summarize the complete junction I-V characteristic.

  8. Reverse Breakdown

  10. Zener Breakdown

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This chapter extends junction physics to metal-semiconductor interfaces, covering work function, electron affinity, and barrier height as the basis for Schottky barriers, Schottky diodes, and ohmic contacts. Students then study the MOS capacitor, tracing how gate voltage moves the semiconductor surface through accumulation, depletion, and strong/weak inversion, culminating in the threshold voltage that defines the on/off behavior of a MOSFET.

docs.title: Optical and Thermal Properties of Semiconductors
Summary
docs.text: This chapter covers how semiconductors absorb light, from the absorption coefficient to photon absorption and photoconductivity, and connects this behavior to optoelectronic devices: photodiodes, solar cells, and light-emitting diodes. Students also learn radiative recombination and briefly consider thermal conductivity and thermal generation rate as complementary non-electrical properties of semiconductor materials.

  7. Light-Emitting Diode
  8. Radiative Recombination
  9. Thermal Conductivity
  10. Thermal Generation Rate

  • Chapter 13: Non-Equilibrium Carriers and Recombination
  • Chapter 15: The P-N Junction Under Bias

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: This capstone chapter connects the course's physics to applied devices: power diodes, rectifier circuits, and varactor diodes, along with an introduction to bipolar transistor and MOSFET basics. Students consider semiconductor device modeling and simulation at a conceptual level, practice constructing band diagrams, and weigh device design trade-offs before completing a capstone project that models the physics of a real semiconductor device.

docs.title: Concepts Covered
docs.text:
This chapter covers the following 10 concepts from the learning graph:

  1. Power Diode
  2. Rectifier Circuit
  3. Varactor Diode
  4. Bipolar Transistor Basics
  5. MOSFET Basics
  6. Semiconductor Device Modeling

  9. Device Design Trade-Offs
  10. Capstone Device Project

docs.text:

  • Chapter 6: Band Structure and the Fermi Level
  • Chapter 7: Intrinsic and Extrinsic Semiconductors

  • Chapter 14: The P-N Junction at Equilibrium
  • Chapter 15: The P-N Junction Under Bias
  • Chapter 16: Metal-Semiconductor and MOS Junctions
  • Chapter 17: Optical and Thermal Properties of Semiconductors

docs.title: Learning Graph for Semiconductor Physics
docs.text:
This section contains the learning graph for this textbook. A learning graph is a graph of concepts used in this textbook. Each concept is represented by a node in a network graph. Concepts are connected by directed edges that indicate what concepts each node depends on before that concept is understood by the student.

A learning graph is the foundational data structure for intelligent textbooks that can recommend learning paths. A learning graph is like a roadmap of concepts to help students arrive at their learning goals.

At the left of the learning graph are prerequisite or foundational concepts. They have no outbound edges. They only have inbound edges for other concepts that depend on understanding these foundational prerequisite concepts. At the far right we have the most advanced concepts in the course. To master these concepts you must understand all the concepts that they point to.

Here are other files used by the learning graph.

docs.title: Course Description
docs.text: We use the Course Description as the source document for the concepts that are included in this course. The course description uses the 2001 Bloom taxonomy to order learning objectives.

docs.title: List of Concepts
docs.text: We use generative AI to convert the course description into a Concept List. Each concept is in the form of a short Title Case label with most labels under 32 characters long. This course has 200 concepts spanning crystal structure, quantum mechanics and band theory, carrier statistics and transport, the p-n junction, and semiconductor devices.

docs.title: Concept Dependency List
docs.text: We next use generative AI to create a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG). DAGs do not have cycles where concepts depend on themselves. We provide the DAG in two formats. One is a CSV file and the other format is a JSON file that uses the vis-network JavaScript library format. The vis-network format uses nodes, edges and metadata elements with edges containing from and to properties. This makes it easy for you to view and edit the learning graph using an editor built with the vis-network tools.

docs.title: Course Description Quality Assessment
docs.text:
This report rates the overall quality of the course description for the purpose of generating a learning graph.

  • Course description fields and content depth analysis
  • Validates course description has sufficient depth for generating 200 concepts
  • Compares course description against similar courses
  • Identifies content gaps and strengths
  • Suggests areas of improvement

View the Course Description Quality Assessment

docs.title: Learning Graph Quality Validation
docs.text:
This report gives you an overall assessment of the quality of the learning graph. It uses graph algorithms to look for specific quality patterns in the graph.

  • Graph structure validation - all 200 concepts are connected in a single component
  • DAG validation (0 cycles detected; every dependency references a strictly earlier concept ID)
  • Foundational concepts: 4 entry points (Coulomb's Law, Kinetic Theory of Gases, Photon Energy, Crystal Lattice)
  • Indegree distribution analysis
  • Longest dependency chain: 32 concepts, from Photon Energy through to the Capstone Device Project
  • Connectivity: 100% of nodes connected to the main cluster

View the Learning Graph Quality Validation

docs.title: Concept Taxonomy
docs.text:
In order to see patterns in the learning graph, it is useful to assign colors to each concept based on the concept type. We use generative AI to create about a dozen categories for our concepts and then place each concept into a single primary classifier.

  • A concept classifier taxonomy with 12 categories
  • Category organization - foundational elements first, course capstone project ideas last
  • Balanced categories (4% - 11.5% each)
  • All categories under the 30% threshold
  • Pedagogical flow recommendations
  • Clear 3-5 letter abbreviations for use in the CSV file

View the Concept Taxonomy

docs.title: Taxonomy Distribution
docs.text:
This report shows how many concepts fit into each category of the taxonomy. Our goal is a somewhat balanced taxonomy where each category holds an equal number of concepts. We also don't want any category to contain over 30% of our concepts.

  • Statistical breakdown
  • Detailed concept listing by category
  • Visual distribution table
  • Balance verification

View the Taxonomy Distribution Report

docs.title: Concept Taxonomy
docs.text:
This taxonomy organizes the 200 concepts in the Semiconductor Physics learning graph into 12 categories, each evenly distributed to avoid any single category dominating the graph.

Category TaxonomyID Description Physics & Math Foundations FOUND Prerequisite physics and quantum mechanics concepts (electrostatics, thermal physics, wave-particle duality, the Schrödinger equation) that underlie the rest of the course. Crystal Structure & Bonding CRYS Crystalline solids: lattice types, unit cells, Miller indices, and the bonding that holds semiconductor crystals together. Quantum Mechanics & Band Theory QMBT How periodic crystal potentials give rise to energy bands, band gaps, and the E-k relationships that distinguish metals, insulators, and semiconductors. Semiconductor Materials & Doping MATL Intrinsic vs. extrinsic materials, donor/acceptor doping, ionization, and the temperature regimes of doped semiconductors. Carrier Statistics STAT Fermi-Dirac statistics, density of states, and the equations governing electron and hole concentrations at equilibrium. Carrier Transport TRAN Drift and diffusion current, mobility, scattering, conductivity, and related transport phenomena (Hall effect, Einstein relation). Non-Equilibrium Carriers NEQ Excess carrier generation and recombination mechanisms, carrier lifetime, and the continuity equation governing non-equilibrium carrier profiles. P-N Junction PNJ The physics of the p-n junction: depletion region, built-in potential, capacitance, the ideal diode equation, and breakdown mechanisms. Metal-Semiconductor Junctions MSJ Schottky barriers, ohmic contacts, and the physics of metal-semiconductor interfaces. MOS Capacitor & Surfaces MOS The MOS capacitor structure, surface conditions (accumulation, depletion, inversion), and threshold voltage — the physical basis of the MOSFET. Optical & Thermal Properties OPT Light absorption, photogeneration, and the optoelectronic and thermal properties of semiconductors. Devices & Applications DEV Applied device concepts (diodes, transistors, device modeling) and the capstone project connecting physics to real device performance.

docs.title: Category Distribution
docs.text: See taxonomy-distribution.md for the full concept-count breakdown per category.

Remember Level 10 10 4 specific, measurable outcomes Understand Level 10 10 4 specific, measurable outcomes Apply Level 10 10 4 specific, measurable outcomes Analyze Level 10 10 4 specific, measurable outcomes Evaluate Level 10 10 4 specific, measurable outcomes Create Level 10 10 4 outcomes including a defined capstone project Descriptive Context 5 5 "Course Importance" section explains relevance

docs.title: Gap Analysis
docs.text: No elements scored below full points. All required sections are present and specific.

docs.title: Improvement Suggestions
docs.text:
None required to proceed. Optional enhancements for later iterations:

  • Consider adding a rough chapter-count target (e.g., 10-12 chapters) once the learning graph is generated, to help book-chapter-generator distribute concepts evenly.
  • If the course later grows to include compound semiconductors or device fabrication in more depth, revisit the "Topics Not Covered" list to keep scope boundaries accurate.

docs.title: Concept Generation Readiness
docs.text: The 11 main topics each span multiple distinct sub-concepts (e.g., "Carrier statistics" alone implies density of states, Fermi-Dirac distribution, effective density of states, Fermi level, intrinsic carrier concentration, mass-action law), and the six Bloom's Taxonomy levels are populated with specific, concept-rich outcomes. This description should comfortably support generation of 200+ concepts for the learning graph.

docs.title: Learning Graph Generator Session Log
docs.text:
• Skill Version: 0.03
  • Date: 2026-07-22
  • Course: Semiconductor Physics (undergrad junior EE/Applied Physics)

docs.title: Steps Performed
docs.text:
1. Course description assessment — skipped re-analysis; existing quality_score: 100 in docs/course-description.md frontmatter was above the 85 threshold.
  2. Concept generation — generated 200 Title Case concept labels (max 32 chars each), organized across 12 pedagogical topic clusters. Saved to concept-list.md.
  3. Dependency graph — hand-authored a 200-node DAG in learning-graph.csv. Every dependency references a strictly smaller ConceptID than its dependent, which mathematically guarantees acyclicity by construction (verified programmatically — zero violations).
  4. Quality validation — ran analyze-graph.py (script version unlabeled, as shipped in skill v0.03).
  5. Cycles detected: 0 (verified independently via DFS)
  6. Known script bug: the "Valid DAG Structure" check in verify_dag() seeds Kahn's-algorithm queue using an inverted indegree calculation (it starts from orphaned/leaf nodes instead of foundational/root nodes), so it always reports "❌ No" even on a provably acyclic graph. This was confirmed by manually checking that every Dependencies entry references a smaller ConceptID, which makes a cycle impossible. Treated as a false negative, not a data defect.
  7. 4 foundational (zero-dependency) concepts; 1 connected component (all 200 nodes reachable); longest chain 32 concepts.
  8. Did one manual pass adding 8 extra cross-links to reduce orphaned (never-referenced) nodes from 60 → 51, connecting concepts like Silicon, Germanium, Gallium Arsenide, Insulator Band Structure, E-K Diagram, Einstein Relation, Indirect Recombination, and Sheet Resistance forward into later device/application concepts.
  9. Taxonomy — created 12 categories (concept-taxonomy.md), all within 4.0%–11.5% of the 200 concepts (well under the 30% over-representation threshold). Ran taxonomy-distribution.py to confirm.
  10. JSON generation — patched the local copy of csv-to-json.py to add classifierName entries for this course's 12 TaxonomyIDs (CRYS, QMBT, MATL, STAT, TRAN, NEQ, PNJ, MSJ, MOS, OPT, DEV, plus FOUND), since the shipped script's default name/color maps only covered a different skill's taxonomy set and numeric IDs — without the patch, groups would have rendered with raw abbreviations instead of descriptive names. Created color-config.json mapping each TaxonomyID to a distinct named pastel color. Ran csv-to-json.py learning-graph.csv learning-graph.json color-config.json metadata.json (script version 0.02) → 200 nodes, 329 edges, 12 groups.
  11. Schema validation — ran validate-learning-graph.py; it failed because the bundled learning-graph-schema.json expects groups.*.color to be a nested object with a lowercase-only color-name pattern, while both the SKILL.md documentation and the csv-to-json.py generator itself produce a flat string (e.g. "color": "LightCoral"). This looks like a stale/out-of-sync schema file in the skill package rather than an error in the generated data. Kept the flat format that matches the documented spec and the generator's actual output.
  12. Taxonomy distribution report — generated via taxonomy-distribution.py, no script issues.
  13. Index page — generated index.md from index-template.md, filled in actual metrics (4 foundational concepts, 100% connectivity, chain length 32, 12 categories).

docs.title: Known Issues to Revisit
docs.text:
• analyze-graph.py's DAG-validity check has an inverted-indegree bug (false negative on a provably acyclic graph).
  • learning-graph-schema.json disagrees with csv-to-json.py's actual output format for the groups.*.color field.
  • 51 concepts remain orphaned (nothing depends on them) — the majority are legitimately terminal/applied leaf concepts (e.g., breakdown mechanisms, specific devices, contrast facts like Ionic/Metallic Bond), which is expected for a well-formed graph, but worth a second look during chapter-outline generation.
